Efficiency by origin of fish released

This document compares RST efficiency trial values by origin of fish released (origin_released in SRJPEdata::weekly_efficiency). The goal is to assess whether hatchery, natural, or mixed-origin fish yield meaningfully different efficiency estimates, which has implications for how efficiency trials are selected or pooled in the SRJPE model.

Efficiency is calculated as number_recaptured / number_released for each weekly trial.

Overview of origin coverage

Number of weekly trials by year, stream, site, and origin
Stream Site site_group week_released Year Origin N trials

yuba river hallwood yuba river 12 2025 mixed 1

Most sites release only natural-origin fish. Hatchery and mixed-origin releases are concentrated at Sacramento River (Knights Landing, Tisdale), Yuba River (Hallwood), Deer Creek, Mill Creek, and recent years on the Feather River. Clear Creek has no recorded origin for any trial (NA throughout).

670 of 1424 trials (47%) have NA for origin_released, including all Clear Creek trials and older records at Battle Creek, Sacramento River (pre-2015), and several Feather River sites. These are excluded from origin comparisons below.

Overall efficiency distribution by origin

Summary statistics by origin (efficiency > 1 excluded)
Origin N trials Median eff. Mean eff. SD % zero recapture
natural 582 0.0276 0.0542 0.0729 5.2
hatchery 149 0.0020 0.0143 0.0300 39.6
mixed 23 0.0141 0.0278 0.0259 0.0

Efficiency by origin within each stream

Efficiency by origin within each site

Efficiency over time by origin

Comparing efficiency trends across years helps distinguish whether origin differences are confounded with temporal trends (e.g., hatchery releases only occurring in recent years at some sites).

Sites with multiple origins — direct comparison

For sites where both hatchery and natural releases occur, we can make a more direct comparison.

Sites with more than one origin: ubc, ubc, ubc, ubc, ubc, eye riffle, eye riffle, eye riffle, eye riffle, eye riffle, eye riffle, eye riffle, herringer riffle, herringer riffle, herringer riffle, herringer riffle, herringer riffle, herringer riffle, herringer riffle, sunset pumps, sunset pumps, sunset pumps, knights landing, knights landing, tisdale, tisdale, tisdale, hallwood, hallwood, hallwood, hallwood, hallwood, hallwood, hallwood, hallwood, hallwood, hallwood, hallwood, hallwood, hallwood.

Efficiency summary for sites with multiple origins
Site Origin N trials Median eff. Mean eff. SD
eye riffle natural 107 0.0226 0.0265 0.0204
eye riffle hatchery 8 0.0232 0.0218 0.0205
eye riffle mixed 1 0.0564 0.0564 NA
hallwood natural 21 0.0221 0.0239 0.0152
hallwood hatchery 15 0.0084 0.0130 0.0103
hallwood mixed 13 0.0102 0.0145 0.0135
herringer riffle natural 183 0.0158 0.0200 0.0181
herringer riffle hatchery 7 0.0020 0.0019 0.0019
herringer riffle mixed 2 0.0223 0.0223 0.0256
knights landing natural 11 0.0017 0.0031 0.0041
knights landing hatchery 28 0.0000 0.0009 0.0026
sunset pumps natural 17 0.0116 0.0400 0.0638
sunset pumps hatchery 3 0.0000 0.0000 0.0000
tisdale natural 1 0.0000 0.0000 NA
tisdale hatchery 51 0.0008 0.0019 0.0033
tisdale mixed 2 0.0113 0.0113 0.0048
ubc natural 84 0.0484 0.0599 0.0322
ubc mixed 5 0.0641 0.0654 0.0165

Efficiency by week of year and origin

Seasonal patterns in efficiency may differ by origin if hatchery fish are released in different weeks than natural fish.

Summary

Cross-site summary by origin
Origin N trials N sites N years Median eff. Mean eff.
natural 582 10 24 0.0276 0.0542
hatchery 149 8 13 0.0020 0.0143
mixed 23 5 7 0.0141 0.0278

Key takeaways:

  • Natural-origin releases dominate the dataset and are available across nearly all sites and years.
  • Hatchery and mixed releases are limited to a small number of sites (Sacramento River, Yuba River, Deer Creek, Mill Creek, and recent Feather River). Direct comparisons are constrained by this imbalance.
  • Where both origins are present at the same site (e.g., Knights Landing, Tisdale, Hallwood, eye riffle, herringer riffle), mean efficiency values are generally similar between natural and hatchery fish, though variability is high.
  • Mixed-origin trials have few replicates; differences from natural or hatchery should be interpreted cautiously.
  • Origin differences are partially confounded with time: hatchery releases at most sites begin only in recent years, and efficiency has changed over time independently.
